title: Quantum-Battery-Powered Geometric Landau-Zener Interferometry
abstract: Classical microwave drives are usually treated as ideal phase-coherent work sources for superconducting-qubit control. What if such a drive is replaced by a finite quantum battery. As a demanding benchmark, we consider echo-refocused geometric Landau--Zener interferometry powered by a single quantized bosonic mode. The qubit--battery dynamics are described by a Jaynes--Cummings Hamiltonian, while the echo pulse is retained as a qubit-only refocusing operation that cancels the dynamical phase. In the macroscopic coherent-state limit, the usual classical geometric interferometer is recovered. At finite mean photon number, however, the Jaynes--Cummings coupling generates photon-number-resolved avoided crossings with gaps $Ω_n=2g\sqrt{n}$. The qubit-only echo redistributes amplitudes between neighboring excitation sectors, so the finite-battery protocol is not a single classical interferometer but a coherent sector-resolved quantum evolution. This produces contrast loss, interferogram distortions, and measurable battery back-action. We further show that reducing photon-number fluctuations alone is not sufficient: geometric control requires a first-order phase reference. Geometric Landau--Zener interferometry therefore provides a practical benchmark for certifying phase-coherent quantum-battery energy.
summary: The paper examines the performance of a geometric Landau-Zener interferometer powered by a finite quantum battery instead of a classical drive. It demonstrates that maintaining a coherent phase reference is more critical for interference contrast than reducing photon-number fluctuations. Consequently, standard coherent states outperform squeezed states for high-fidelity geometric control.
main_problem: Investigating whether a finite quantum battery can replace classical phase-coherent microwave drives for superconducting qubit control and determining if energy alone or phase-coherent energy is the critical resource for geometric control.
main_result: The study finds that reducing photon-number fluctuations via squeezing is insufficient for improving interference contrast because the loss of the coherent phase-reference strength outweighs the benefits of reduced gap broadening; coherent states provide the optimal balance.
method: Full density-matrix evolution using a Lindblad master equation and an adaptive eighth-order Runge-Kutta solver to account for qubit-battery dynamics and back-action.
model_or_system: A two-level system (qubit) coupled to a single quantized bosonic mode (the quantum battery) via a Jaynes-Cummings Hamiltonian, undergoing a Landau-Zener-Stueckelberg interferometry protocol with an echo pulse.
key_observables: Final excited-state population (interferogram contrast), fringe contrast (C), photon-number change (battery back-action), and the coherent phase-reference fraction (eta_coh).
important_parameters: Mean photon number (n_bar), photon-number variance, coupling strength (g), and the coherent fraction (eta_coh); regimes include the macroscopic coherent-state limit and the few-quanta quantum regime.
main_assumptions: The echo pulse is treated as a qubit-only operation that does not act on the battery; the rotating-wave approximation is applied.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows a schematic of photon-number-resolved Landau-Zener passages and dressed adiabatic branches; Figure 3 compares performance across different battery states.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of replacing classical drives with quantum batteries, defines the Jaynes-Cummings model and the GLZI protocol, analyzes the impact of photon-number fluctuations and gap broadening, compares various battery states (coherent vs. squeezed), and concludes with the necessity of a phase reference.
why_interesting: This paper is highly relevant to researchers in open quantum systems and quantum optics as it explores the fundamental limits of using non-classical energy reservoirs for coherent control and quantifies the trade-off between energy fluctuations and phase coherence.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 2: Finite-battery geometric Landau–Zener in- terference. The final excited-state population Pe = Tr[ρ(τC)|e⟩⟨e|] is shown as a function of the geometric phase θgeo and the Landau–Zener sweep time τp. (a) Classical-drive limit, obtained from the corresponding transverse-field Hamiltonian with the same mean gap Ω/2π = 20 MHz. (b) Full quantum-battery evolution for a coherent battery with ¯n = 5, using g = Ω/(2√¯n). Both panels use the same color scale. The finite bat- tery preserves the echo-protected geometric fringe pat- tern, but the extrema are softened and weakly distorted by the photon-number-resolved gaps Ωn = Ω p

FIG. 3: Quantum-to-classical recovery and battery back- action. (a) Fringe contrast C = maxθgeo Pe −minθgeo Pe for a coherent quantum battery as a function of the mean photon number ¯n. The dashed line gives the correspond- ing classical-drive limit with the same qubit relaxation and dephasing rates. The inset shows the contrast deficit Ccl−C versus 1/¯n, illustrating the approach to the classi- cal limit as photon-number fluctuations become relatively small. (b) Battery back-action, quantified by the photon- number change ∆n = ⟨n⟩i−⟨n⟩f, averaged over θgeo. Er- ror bars denote the standard deviation over the geomet- ric phase, not numerical uncertainty. The mean photon- number change...

FIG. 1: Coherent quantum-battery fringes for different mean photon numbers. The final excited-state population Pe is shown as a function of the geometric phase θgeo for coherent batteries with ¯n = 0.5, 1, 2, 5, 10, together with the classical-drive limit. All curves are obtained from the full qubit–battery master equation, with the coupling calibrated as g = Ω/(2√¯n) so that the central photon-number sector has the same nominal gap Ω. As ¯n increases, the finite- battery fringe approaches the classical response. At small ¯n, the fringe contrast is reduced because the coherent state contains a broad distribution of photon-number components, each generating a different Landau–Zener gap...

FIG. 2: Finite-coherent-battery heatmaps at few-quanta occupation. The final excited-state population Pe is shown as a function of the geometric phase θgeo and the Landau–Zener sweep time τp, using the full qubit–battery master equation. Panels show coherent batteries with (a) ¯n = 2 and (b) ¯n = 5, with the coupling calibrated as g = Ω/(2√¯n) in each case so that the central photon-number sector has the same nominal gap Ω. All other parameters are the same as in the main text: Ω/2π = 20 MHz, δ0/2π = 100 MHz, τC = 100 ns, T1 = 118 ns, T2 = 157 ns, κ = 10−4 ns−1, and ¯nth = 0.
